title: Rounds bundled critical / panic value table
description: Deterministic critical (panic) thresholds that drive the app's emergency-escalation banner (ARCHITECTURE.md §6, Principle 6). The Swift critical-value detector compares each extracted sidecar marker against this table AND the lab's own printed panic flags. Stating that a value is at/beyond one of these thresholds is PRIMARY-DATA ARITHMETIC, exempt from the sources-only rule (Principle 2). It is NOT a diagnosis and NOT a literature claim.
disclaimer: These are conservative, widely-cited adult panic/critical-value thresholds intended ONLY to trigger an 'this may need urgent attention' prompt — not to diagnose, grade severity, or replace a clinician. Thresholds vary by laboratory, assay, age, sex, pregnancy, and clinical context. Pediatric and neonatal values differ substantially and are out of scope for v1. The lab's own reported reference range and panic flags always take precedence over this table. When a unit on a report does not match a marker's expected unit here, the detector must REFUSE to compare rather than risk a unit-mismatch error (e.g. mmol/L vs mg/dL).
sourceNote: Thresholds compiled from commonly published U.S. clinical-laboratory critical-value (panic-value) policies and reference texts, including ranges consistent with the College of American Pathologists (CAP) critical-value surveys, Tietz Textbook of Clinical Chemistry, and standard hospital laboratory panic-value lists. Values are representative consensus figures; individual laboratories set their own action thresholds. No patient data was used to build this table.

matching.note: Match a sidecar marker to an entry by `loincHints` first, then by case-insensitive name/alias. Only evaluate when the marker's canonical unit equals the entry's `unit` (or an entry in `acceptedUnits`). If units differ and no safe conversion exists, set comparison='refused_unit_mismatch' and do NOT flag.
matching.comparison: A value V triggers a critical flag when V <= criticalLow (if criticalLow is non-null) OR V >= criticalHigh (if criticalHigh is non-null).

markers.name: Potassium
markers.rationale: Severe hypo-/hyperkalemia risks life-threatening cardiac arrhythmia.
markers.sourceNote: Common lab panic range; CAP/Tietz consensus (criticalLow ~2.5-3.0, criticalHigh ~6.0-6.5).

markers.name: Sodium
markers.rationale: Severe hypo-/hypernatremia risks seizures, cerebral edema, or osmotic demyelination.
markers.sourceNote: Typical panic thresholds (criticalLow ~120-125, criticalHigh ~155-160).

markers.name: Glucose
markers.rationale: Severe hypoglycemia risks coma; severe hyperglycemia risks DKA/HHS.
markers.sourceNote: Common panic values (criticalLow ~40-50 mg/dL, criticalHigh ~400-500 mg/dL). For mmol/L reports the detector must convert or refuse; 50 mg/dL ≈ 2.8 mmol/L, 450 mg/dL ≈ 25 mmol/L.

markers.name: Glucose (mmol/L)
markers.rationale: SI-unit equivalent of the glucose panic range for labs reporting in mmol/L.
markers.sourceNote: 2.8 mmol/L ≈ 50 mg/dL; 25 mmol/L ≈ 450 mg/dL.

markers.name: Hemoglobin
markers.rationale: Severe anemia risks tissue hypoxia / cardiac strain; very high Hgb risks hyperviscosity.
markers.sourceNote: Common panic threshold criticalLow ~6.5-7.0 g/dL, criticalHigh ~18-20 g/dL. For g/L reports divide by 10 (70 g/L = 7.0 g/dL).

markers.name: Hemoglobin (g/L)
markers.rationale: SI-unit equivalent for labs reporting hemoglobin in g/L.

markers.name: Platelets
markers.rationale: Severe thrombocytopenia risks spontaneous bleeding; extreme thrombocytosis risks thrombosis.
markers.sourceNote: Common panic threshold criticalLow ~20-30 x10^9/L, criticalHigh ~1000 x10^9/L. K/uL and x10^9/L are numerically equal.

markers.name: Calcium
markers.rationale: Severe hypo-/hypercalcemia risks tetany, seizures, arrhythmia, or coma.
markers.sourceNote: Typical panic thresholds criticalLow ~6.0-6.5 mg/dL, criticalHigh ~13.0-14.0 mg/dL. 6.0 mg/dL ≈ 1.5 mmol/L; 13 mg/dL ≈ 3.25 mmol/L.

markers.name: Calcium (mmol/L)
markers.rationale: SI-unit equivalent of the calcium panic range.
markers.sourceNote: 1.5 mmol/L ≈ 6.0 mg/dL; 3.25 mmol/L ≈ 13 mg/dL.

markers.name: White blood cell count
markers.rationale: Severe leukopenia risks life-threatening infection; very high WBC may indicate leukemia/sepsis.
markers.sourceNote: Common panic threshold criticalLow ~2.0 x10^9/L, criticalHigh ~30-50 x10^9/L.

markers.name: INR
markers.rationale: INR >= 5 markedly elevates major bleeding risk, especially on anticoagulation.
markers.sourceNote: Common critical threshold criticalHigh ~4.5-5.0.

markers.name: Troponin I
markers.rationale: An elevated troponin above the assay's 99th-percentile cutoff can indicate acute myocardial injury — interpret only with the lab's own assay-specific cutoff and clinical context.
markers.sourceNote: Cutoffs are HIGHLY assay-specific (contemporary cTnI 99th percentile often ~0.03-0.04 ng/mL; high-sensitivity assays report in ng/L with different cutoffs). The detector should defer to the lab's printed reference/cutoff when present; this value is a conservative fallback trigger only.

markers.name: Troponin T (high-sensitivity)
markers.rationale: hs-cTnT above the sex-specific 99th-percentile cutoff may indicate acute myocardial injury; interpret with the lab's assay-specific cutoff and serial change.
markers.sourceNote: Representative hs-cTnT 99th-percentile cutoff (~14-22 ng/L); a value of 52 ng/L is a conservative single-sample trigger. Defer to the lab's printed cutoff.

markers.name: Magnesium
markers.rationale: Severe hypo-/hypermagnesemia risks arrhythmia, neuromuscular collapse, and respiratory depression.
markers.sourceNote: Common panic thresholds criticalLow ~1.0 mg/dL, criticalHigh ~4.7-9.0 mg/dL.

markers.name: Phosphorus
markers.rationale: Severe hypophosphatemia risks respiratory/muscle failure; severe hyperphosphatemia risks metastatic calcification.
markers.sourceNote: Common panic thresholds criticalLow ~1.0 mg/dL, criticalHigh ~8.0-9.0 mg/dL.

markers.name: Bicarbonate
markers.rationale: Very low bicarbonate signals severe metabolic acidosis; very high signals severe alkalosis.
markers.sourceNote: Common panic thresholds criticalLow ~10 mmol/L, criticalHigh ~40 mmol/L.

markers.name: Creatinine
markers.rationale: A markedly elevated creatinine suggests severe acute or chronic kidney impairment warranting prompt evaluation; absolute thresholds are less standardized than electrolytes.
markers.sourceNote: Representative critical-high ~7.4 mg/dL (≈654 umol/L); many labs flag on rate-of-rise rather than an absolute value. Severity 'attention' rather than 'urgent' because trend/context matters.

markers.name: Bilirubin (neonatal note)
markers.rationale: A high total bilirubin in an adult warrants evaluation for hepatobiliary disease; neonatal kernicterus thresholds are entirely different and OUT OF SCOPE for v1.
markers.sourceNote: Adult attention threshold ~15 mg/dL; this entry is NOT valid for neonates. Pediatric/neonatal bilirubin nomograms are explicitly excluded in v1.

markers.name: Lactate
markers.rationale: Lactate >= 4 mmol/L is associated with severe sepsis/shock and high mortality.
markers.sourceNote: Sepsis literature commonly uses a >= 4 mmol/L critical threshold; >= 2 mmol/L is an early warning.

markers.name: pH (arterial blood gas)
markers.rationale: Severe acidemia/alkalemia is immediately life-threatening.
markers.sourceNote: Common panic thresholds criticalLow ~7.20, criticalHigh ~7.60.
